---
title: "Introduction to Particle Physics II: The Standard Model and Neutrinos"
subtitle: "Mini-course · 75 min"
author: "Dmitry V. Naumov"
institute: "Joint Institute for Nuclear Research"
date: "June 2026"
---

# Orientation

## Lecture Map

::: {.timing}
::: {.time}
0-7 min
:::
::: {}
What we take from Lecture 1.
:::
::: {.time}
7-22 min
:::
::: {}
The fermion content of the Standard Model.
:::
::: {.time}
22-37 min
:::
::: {}
Gauge structure: \(SU(3)_c\times SU(2)_L\times U(1)_Y\).
:::
::: {.time}
37-50 min
:::
::: {}
Weak interactions and neutrino processes.
:::
::: {.time}
50-60 min
:::
::: {}
The Higgs mechanism and masses.
:::
::: {.time}
60-75 min
:::
::: {}
Neutrino oscillations and the limits of the minimal SM.
:::
:::

## What We Keep from Lecture 1

We will use four ideas:

::: {.compact}
- particles are quanta of fields;
- fermions and bosons differ by spin and statistics;
- interactions are encoded in vertices and amplitudes;
- experiments measure cross sections, decay rates, spectra, and event rates.
:::

::: {.takeaway}
Now we assemble this language into a specific quantum field theory: the Standard Model.
:::

# Standard Model

## What the Standard Model Is

The Standard Model is not just a table of particles.

It is a quantum field theory with:

::: {.compact}
- a set of matter fields;
- gauge symmetry;
- gauge interactions;
- the Higgs field;
- parameters for masses and mixings.
:::

Its predictions have been tested with high precision across a wide range of processes.

## Not a Table, but a Structure

A particle table answers "who is who."

A theory answers:

::: {.compact}
- which fields exist;
- how they transform under symmetries;
- which interactions are allowed;
- which masses appear after symmetry breaking;
- which processes are forbidden or suppressed.
:::

Neutrinos enter this structure naturally, but their masses do not enter the minimal version.

# Fermion Content

## Three Generations

Standard Model fermions are organized into three generations.

Each generation contains:

::: {.compact}
- a charged lepton;
- a neutrino;
- an up-type quark;
- a down-type quark.
:::

The generation pattern repeats, but particle masses differ dramatically.

## Lepton Doublets

Left-handed leptons enter weak doublets:

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
\nu_e\\ e
\end{pmatrix}_L,
\quad
\begin{pmatrix}
\nu_\mu\\ \mu
\end{pmatrix}_L,
\quad
\begin{pmatrix}
\nu_\tau\\ \tau
\end{pmatrix}_L.
$$

The charged current changes the upper and lower members of a doublet.

This is why neutrino flavor is identified through the associated charged lepton.

## Quark Doublets

Left-handed quarks also enter weak doublets:

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
u\\ d
\end{pmatrix}_L,
\quad
\begin{pmatrix}
c\\ s
\end{pmatrix}_L,
\quad
\begin{pmatrix}
t\\ b
\end{pmatrix}_L.
$$

In the real theory, weak and mass bases for quarks are not identical.

This leads to CKM mixing.

## Charges Table

| Particles | \(Q\) | Color | Interactions |
|---|---:|---|---|
| \(\nu_e,\nu_\mu,\nu_\tau\) | \(0\) | no | weak |
| \(e,\mu,\tau\) | \(-1\) | no | EM, weak |
| \(u,c,t\) | \(+2/3\) | yes | strong, EM, weak |
| \(d,s,b\) | \(-1/3\) | yes | strong, EM, weak |

This table already tells us which detector channels are possible.

## Why Generations Are Puzzling

The Standard Model describes generations but does not explain their origin.

Open questions:

::: {.compact}
- Why three generations?
- Why such different masses?
- Why are quark and neutrino mixing patterns so different?
- Why are neutrinos so light?
:::

These are not cosmetic questions. They point to the structure of flavor.

# Gauge Structure

## Gauge Symmetry Statement

The Standard Model gauge group is:

$$
SU(3)_c \times SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y.
$$

This compact expression states which local symmetries define the interactions.

One does not need the full group theory machinery to extract the physical meaning.

## \(SU(3)_c\)

$$
SU(3)_c
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
\text{QCD, gluons, color}.
$$

Quarks carry color charge.

Gluons also carry color, so the strong interaction is nonlinear.

This is one key difference between QCD and electrodynamics.

## Color and Gluons

Physical hadrons must be color singlets.

Examples:

$$
q\bar q
\quad\text{mesons},
\qquad
qqq
\quad\text{baryons}.
$$

In detectors, color appears not as a visible color, but as hadronic showers, jets, and nuclear final states.

## Confinement

Confinement means that isolated colored particles are not observed as asymptotic states.

For this introductory course:

::: {.compact}
- quarks and gluons are Standard Model fields;
- free quarks do not exit the detector as isolated particles;
- detectors see hadrons and their cascades.
:::

This matters for neutrino-nucleon scattering:

$$
\nu+N\to \ell+X.
$$

## Electroweak Group

$$
SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
\text{electroweak interaction}.
$$

Before symmetry breaking, the natural fields are not the familiar photon, \(W\), and \(Z\) in their final form.

After electroweak symmetry breaking we observe:

$$
\gamma,\quad W^\pm,\quad Z.
$$

## Electroweak Symmetry Breaking

Schematically:

$$
SU(2)_L \times U(1)_Y
\to
U(1)_{\rm EM}.
$$

Physical consequences:

::: {.compact}
- the photon remains massless;
- \(W\) and \(Z\) become massive;
- the weak interaction becomes short-range;
- the electromagnetic interaction remains long-range.
:::

## Gauge Bosons Table

| Interaction | Gauge bosons | Coupled charge | Key feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| Strong | \(g\) | color | confinement |
| Electromagnetic | \(\gamma\) | electric charge | long range |
| Weak charged current | \(W^\pm\) | weak isospin | changes doublet member |
| Weak neutral current | \(Z\) | electroweak charge | no electric charge exchange |

## Photon, W, Z

Photon:

$$
m_\gamma=0,
\qquad
\text{long-range EM}.
$$

Weak bosons:

$$
m_W,\ m_Z \ne 0,
\qquad
\text{short-range weak interaction}.
$$

The masses of \(W\) and \(Z\) are why weak interactions are weak at low energies.

## Weak Interaction Is Short Range

Roughly, a mediator of mass \(M\) gives a range of order:

$$
R\sim \frac{1}{M}.
$$

Since \(W\) and \(Z\) are heavy, weak processes are rare at low energies.

For neutrinos, this rarity is the detector problem.

::: {.takeaway}
Neutrinos pass through matter not because they are "small," but because their cross sections are small.
:::

# Weak Interaction and Neutrinos

## Charged Current

The charged current connects a neutrino and a charged lepton:

$$
\nu_\ell + W^- \leftrightarrow \ell^-.
$$

Equivalently for antiparticles:

$$
\bar\nu_\ell + W^+ \leftrightarrow \ell^+.
$$

The charged current provides the most flavor-specific signatures.

## Neutral Current

The neutral current is:

$$
\nu_\ell + Z \leftrightarrow \nu_\ell.
$$

It does not exchange electric charge.

For detector topology this means:

::: {.compact}
- no final-state charged lepton is produced at the primary vertex;
- flavor information is usually weaker;
- the visible signal is often hadronic or electromagnetic recoil light.
:::

## Neutrino Examples

Charged-current examples:

$$
\nu_e+n\to p+e^-,
$$

$$
\bar\nu_e+p\to n+e^+,
$$

$$
\nu_\mu+N\to\mu^-+X.
$$

Neutral-current example:

$$
\nu+N\to\nu+X.
$$

## Inverse Beta Decay

$$
\bar\nu_e+p\to n+e^+.
$$

This channel is especially important in MeV-scale detector physics.

The signature often has two parts:

::: {.compact}
- a prompt signal from \(e^+\);
- a delayed signal from neutron capture.
:::

For the mini-course it is a useful example of how the interaction channel defines the event signature.

## Neutrino-Nucleon Charged Current

For GeV-TeV energies:

$$
\nu_\mu+N\to\mu^-+X,
\qquad
\bar\nu_\mu+N\to\mu^++X.
$$

The final muon can produce a long track.

The hadronic system \(X\) carries part of the energy and complicates reconstruction.

## Neutral-Current Event

Neutral-current process:

$$
\nu+N\to\nu+X.
$$

The final-state neutrino escapes with part of the energy.

Therefore:

::: {.compact}
- reconstructed energy is not the full incoming neutrino energy;
- flavor tagging is weak;
- the event often appears cascade-like.
:::

## Event Topologies

| Channel | Typical visible topology | Flavor information |
|---|---|---|
| \(\nu_\mu\) CC | long track + hadronic light | strong |
| \(\nu_e\) CC | cascade-like | strong but topology-limited |
| \(\nu_\tau\) CC | tau signatures at high energy | energy-dependent |
| NC | cascade-like, missing energy | weak |

This is an orientation table. Details depend on energy, medium, and reconstruction.

## Interaction Length

The mean interaction length is:

$$
L_{\rm int}
\sim
\frac{1}{n\sigma}.
$$

Here \(n\) is the target number density and \(\sigma\) is the cross section.

A small cross section means an enormous interaction length.

## Why Detectors Are Large

The event count scales as:

$$
N_{\rm events}
=
\Phi\sigma N_{\rm target}T\epsilon.
$$

In neutrino physics one cannot simply make the source brighter.

One needs:

::: {.compact}
- large volume;
- long exposure;
- low backgrounds;
- stable reconstruction.
:::

# Higgs and Masses

## Higgs Field and Vacuum Expectation Value

The Higgs field has a nonzero vacuum expectation value:

$$
\langle H\rangle = \frac{v}{\sqrt{2}},
\qquad
v\simeq 246\ \mathrm{GeV}.
$$

This is a statement about the vacuum of the theory, not about mechanical friction in a medium.

Without popular analogies: the spectrum of excitations changes after symmetry breaking.

## W and Z Masses

After electroweak symmetry breaking:

::: {.compact}
- \(W^\pm\) and \(Z\) get masses;
- the photon remains massless;
- the weak interaction becomes short-range;
- the electromagnetic interaction remains long-range.
:::

This connects the symmetry structure to observable cross sections.

## Fermion Masses and Yukawa Couplings

For charged fermions:

$$
m_f=\frac{y_f v}{\sqrt{2}}.
$$

Here \(y_f\) is a Yukawa coupling.

::: {.takeaway}
The Standard Model describes fermion masses through Yukawa couplings, but it does not explain why those couplings have their observed values.
:::

## Mass Hierarchy

Yukawa couplings differ by many orders of magnitude.

The Standard Model takes this as a set of parameters.

Examples of open questions:

::: {.compact}
- why \(m_t\) is close to the electroweak scale;
- why \(m_e\) is much smaller;
- why neutrino masses are even smaller;
- why mixing in quark and lepton sectors is so different.
:::

## Minimal SM Neutrinos Are Massless

In the minimal Standard Model:

::: {.compact}
- left-handed neutrino fields appear in lepton doublets;
- no right-handed neutrino fields are included;
- there is no Dirac mass term for neutrinos;
- a Majorana mass term is absent in the minimal renormalizable SM field content.
:::

Consequence:

$$
m_{\nu_i}=0
\quad
\text{in the minimal SM}.
$$

# Neutrino Oscillations

## Flavor and Mass States

Flavor states are produced and detected through weak interactions.

Mass states have definite masses and phase evolution.

The relation is:

$$
|\nu_\alpha\rangle
=
\sum_i U_{\alpha i}^*
|\nu_i\rangle.
$$

Here \(U\) is the PMNS mixing matrix.

## Two-Flavor Oscillation Formula

In the vacuum two-flavor approximation:

$$
P_{\nu_\alpha\to\nu_\beta}
=
\sin^2 2\theta\,
\sin^2
\left(
1.27\,
\frac{\Delta m^2[\mathrm{eV}^2]\,L[\mathrm{km}]}
{E[\mathrm{GeV}]}
\right).
$$

For survival probability:

$$
P_{\nu_\alpha\to\nu_\alpha}=1-P_{\nu_\alpha\to\nu_\beta}.
$$

## Survival Probability

Equivalently:

$$
P_{\nu_\alpha\to\nu_\alpha}
=
1-
\sin^2 2\theta\,
\sin^2
\left(
1.27\,
\frac{\Delta m^2[\mathrm{eV}^2]\,L[\mathrm{km}]}
{E[\mathrm{GeV}]}
\right).
$$

The oscillation phase is controlled by \(L/E\).

## Toy Code Fragment

This is a vacuum two-flavor toy calculation.

```python
import numpy as np
from particle_intro.oscillations import two_flavor_survival_probability

L_over_E = np.logspace(1, 5, 400)
P = two_flavor_survival_probability(
    L_km=L_over_E,
    E_GeV=1.0,
    dm2_eV2=2.5e-3,
    theta=np.pi / 4,
)
```

It does not include matter effects, full three-flavor mixing, or detector smearing.

## \(L/E\) Dependence

The positions of minima are controlled by the phase:

$$
\phi
\simeq
1.27
\frac{\Delta m^2 L}{E}.
$$

Therefore different experiments are sensitive to different \(\Delta m^2\):

::: {.compact}
- short baselines probe larger \(\Delta m^2\);
- long baselines probe smaller \(\Delta m^2\);
- atmospheric neutrinos naturally scan a broad range of \(L/E\).
:::

## Atmospheric Neutrinos and Detector Scale

For atmospheric neutrinos the baseline depends on zenith angle:

::: {.compact}
- down-going: tens of km;
- through Earth: up to the Earth-diameter scale.
:::

At GeV energies this gives strong sensitivity to:

$$
\nu_\mu\to\nu_\tau
\quad
\text{oscillation at atmospheric } \Delta m^2.
$$

Detector-scale point: a large water volume turns rare interactions into tracks and cascades.

## What Oscillations Prove

Oscillations require:

::: {.compact}
- nonzero mass-squared differences;
- mixing between flavor and mass states;
- at least two different neutrino masses.
:::

They do not directly measure the absolute neutrino mass scale.

::: {.takeaway}
Neutrino oscillations are evidence for physics beyond the minimal SM with massless neutrinos.
:::

## Open Neutrino Questions

Key questions:

::: {.compact}
- absolute neutrino mass scale;
- mass ordering;
- Dirac or Majorana nature;
- CP violation in the lepton sector;
- sterile neutrinos, if any;
- connection to the origin of flavor.
:::

Not all of these questions can be answered by oscillation experiments alone.

# Where the SM Ends

## What the SM Does Not Explain

| Fact | Why it is a problem |
|---|---|
| Neutrino masses | minimal SM gives massless neutrinos |
| Dark matter | no suitable SM particle |
| Baryon asymmetry | SM CP violation is not enough |
| Fermion mass hierarchy | Yukawa pattern unexplained |
| Gravity | not part of SM |
| Strong CP problem | \(\theta_{\rm QCD}\) is extremely small |

## SM as an Effective Theory

The Standard Model is not a wrong theory.

It is an extraordinarily precise effective theory within its domain of applicability.

Neutrinos are especially valuable:

::: {.compact}
- they enter the SM through the weak interaction;
- they are experimentally accessible through rare event rates;
- their oscillations require extending the minimal SM.
:::

## Final Checkpoint

After the two lectures, you should be able to connect:

$$
\text{SM fields}
\to
\text{allowed interactions}
\to
\text{cross sections and rates}
\to
\text{detector signatures}.
$$

Control questions:

::: {.compact}
- how a charged current differs from a neutral current;
- why \(W,Z\) are massive while the photon is massless;
- what neutrino oscillations prove;
- why the minimal SM is incomplete.
:::

## Further Reading

::: {.small}
- Particle Data Group, *Review of Particle Physics*.
- Quigg, *Gauge Theories of the Strong, Weak, and Electromagnetic Interactions*.
- Gonzalez-Garcia and Maltoni, *Phenomenology with Massive Neutrinos*.
- Formaggio and Zeller, *From eV to EeV: Neutrino Cross Sections Across Energy Scales*.
:::

::: {.muted}
Bibliographic entries in `references.bib` are intentionally marked `TODO: verify` before final publication.
:::
